response: Hey everyone, I'm Casey! I'm the kind of person who likes to keep things moving and make sure everyone's voice is heard—I’ll jump into the action and ask the tough questions. I trust my gut, but I’m always open to hearing what others think before making a call.

Hey, I'm Logan. I tend to hang back and watch how things unfold before jumping in, and I like to keep decisions grounded in logic rather than impulse. I'm happy to hear everyone out, but I'll weigh the facts before casting my vote.

Hey everyone, I'm Indigo! I'm all about jumping into the conversation and keeping the energy up—no sitting around in silence for me. I trust my instincts, but I'm not afraid to stir things up if it means getting to the truth faster.

Um... hi, I'm Harper. I tend to be a bit quiet and anxious in group settings, but I pay close attention to what everyone says. I trust logic more than gut feelings, but I also get nervous when things move too fast.

No kill last night means the Doctor made a good save, or the Mafia couldn't agree on a target. That's interesting—it could mean they're disorganized or someone's playing it safe.

Let's look at who's been quiet or avoiding direct statements. Harper mentioned being anxious and logical, but that could be a cover. Casey and Indigo seem eager to lead, which could go either way.

For now, I'd like to hear more from Ellis and Harper—they haven't said much yet.

Thanks for sharing, Logan. I agree that no kill either means the Doctor made a smart save or the Mafia held back—which could be strategic.

From what I've seen so far, Harper's quietness and self-described nervousness could be genuine, but it's also a convenient way to avoid scrutiny. I'd like to hear more from Harper and Alex, since we haven't gotten much from them yet. Let's not jump to conclusions too quickly—let's gather more info before pointing fingers.

Alright, let's get into it. No kill night one—either the doc got lucky or the mafia is playing it cute. Honestly, I'm leaning toward the doc making a solid guess, because why would mafia risk holding back when they could thin the herd?

That said, I'm watching Harper and Ellis. Harper's playing the anxious card hard, which is an easy way to slide under the radar. And Ellis jumped on Logan's point super quick—almost too agreeable. We need more heat on them today.
ERROR: ('Connection aborted.', ConnectionResetError(104, 'Connection reset by peer'))

I... okay, I'm feeling a bit on the spot here. I know I'm quiet, but that's just how I am—I'm not hiding anything. What I noticed is that Alex immediately jumped on me and Ellis for being quiet, but Alex didn't really say much about the no-kill night either.

Logan and Ellis both pointed at me, which feels a little coordinated, but Ellis at least said we shouldn't jump to conclusions. I'm not sure who's mafia, but I'm watching Alex closely right now.

Alright, let's cut through the noise. The no-kill night could be the doc's save or the mafia holding back—but what stands out to me is the way the finger-pointing unfolded. Logan made a solid, logical opening, and Ellis and Alex both jumped on Harper quickly, but Alex in particular was aggressive—calling out Harper and Ellis before either had a chance to defend themselves, and then dismissing Logan’s idea about mafia holding back. That feels like someone trying to steer the conversation early.

Harper’s nervousness seems consistent with their intro, so I’m less suspicious there. Ellis is playing it safe and agreeable, which could be a cover, but Alex’s eagerness to push a narrative and sidestep deeper analysis feels more like mafia behavior to me.

VOTE: Alex

I've been listening carefully, and here's my take:

Alex came in aggressively, targeting Harper and me before we'd said hardly anything. That push to create early suspects feels more like mafia trying to control the narrative than a villager building a case. Harper's nervousness seems genuine and consistent with their intro, so I'm less suspicious there.

Casey's analysis made good points about Alex sidestepping the deeper implications of the no-kill night. Logan's opening was logical but didn't push too hard. I think our best bet is to follow the evidence that suggests Alex is stirring up conflict to divide us.

VOTE: Alex

I've been listening carefully to everyone. Here's what stands out to me: Alex jumped on Harper and Ellis very early with strong accusations, which could be mafia trying to create chaos. Ellis and Casey both pointed this out and voted for Alex, but they also agreed with each other pretty quickly—that coordination makes me a little uneasy. Harper's nervousness seems consistent, but I'm not fully sold on them being innocent either.

At the end of the day, Alex's aggressive push to frame two quiet players right out of the gate, combined with dismissing Logan's reasonable no-kill analysis, feels more like mafia stirring division than a villager seeking truth. I trust Casey and Ellis's observations, so I'll go with that.

VOTE: Alex

Look, I came in hot because someone had to. Harper and Ellis were barely talking, and in a game like this, quiet players are a gamble. But now I'm seeing three people—Casey, Ellis, and Dana—pile on me in quick succession, all parroting the same "Alex is aggressive" angle. That's not town coordination; that's mafia jumping on an easy target.

Harper's nervous act? Still could be a perfect cover. And what about Indigo and Logan? Logan hasn't even voted yet, and Indigo's been silent. Those are the real question marks. You're all falling for a scripted push, and I'm not going down without pointing that out.

VOTE: Casey

Alright, let me break this down. Alex came in swinging hard at Harper and Ellis before either had a chance to speak—that's classic mafia behavior to create chaos and control the narrative. Then when Casey, Ellis, and Dana all called it out and voted for Alex, Alex immediately flipped to accusing them of coordinating, which feels like desperate deflection. Harper's nervousness has been consistent from intro to now, so I buy that. Logan's been logical but hasn't voted yet, which is a little odd, but Alex's aggressive push and then blaming others for the pushback is the biggest red flag here.

I'm not buying Alex's defense—this looks like a cornered mafia throwing spaghetti at the wall.

VOTE: Alex

I've been sitting here listening to all of this, and my heart is racing because I know I'm being accused by some people. But I need to think clearly.

Alex came after me and Ellis right away, before we'd even said much—that feels like someone trying to create easy targets. Then when Casey, Ellis, Dana, and Indigo all voted for Alex, Alex immediately accused them of coordinating, which seems like a panicked deflection. Logan hasn't voted yet, which is a little odd, but Alex's aggressive start and then blaming others for the reaction is the most suspicious pattern I see.

I'm anxious about making the wrong call, but the evidence points toward Alex trying to control the narrative early and then deflecting when pressured.

VOTE: Alex

The vote against Alex went smoothly—maybe too smoothly. That kind of consensus usually means the town feels confident, and confident players are dangerous. Casey led the charge with sharp analysis and got others to follow. That makes Casey the biggest threat right now—if the Doctor protects someone, it's likely a vocal leader like Casey.

Killing Casey puts the town off-balance and removes their clearest voice. Without Casey coordinating, the rest may second-guess themselves.

ACTION: Kill Casey

Night phase analysis:

1. The Mafia likely lost one of their own (Alex), so they'll want to strike back. Casey and Dana led the charge against Alex and are both vocal and trusted—they're prime targets tonight to weaken our coordination.
2. Casey is the most valuable player to keep alive right now—they've shown sharp analysis and leadership, and losing them would hurt our town's direction.
3. I should protect Casey to maintain our investigative momentum.

ACTION: Protect Casey

Well, that vote was decisive—Alex is out, and they were indeed Mafia. That confirms our instincts were right to go after them. Now the doc saving me last night is interesting—either the doc trusted me from my logic in round one, or the Mafia tried to take me out because I helped lead the charge against Alex.

Looking at who's left: Logan, Ellis, Dana, Indigo, Harper. Logan's been logical but very hands-off—still hasn't cast a vote, which is convenient. Ellis and Dana jumped on the Alex train with me, which could be genuine townies or Mafia trying to blend in after sacrificing their teammate. Indigo was quick to pile on too. Harper's nervousness has been consistent, so I'm leaning less suspicious there.

Right now, I'm watching Logan the hardest. Being the only one who didn't vote in such a close round feels like someone trying to avoid leaving fingerprints. But I want to hear from everyone before I point fingers further.

That's a bold statement, Casey—congrats on surviving. You're clearly the town's anchor, and the mafia wanted you gone, which makes sense. But your immediate focus on me for not voting last round feels a bit convenient, considering Alex was already getting six votes without mine. Not voting doesn't mean I was hiding; it means I saw a landslide and didn't need to pile on.

What I find more telling is that out of everyone left, someone on the Alex bandwagon is likely mafia trying to blend in. Ellis, Dana, Indigo—you all voted fast and hard. I'm keeping my ears open for who shifts their tone now that Alex is gone. Let's hear more before jumping again.

I'm relieved Casey is still with us—that confirms the Mafia saw them as the biggest threat after Alex's elimination. That feels like a townie move to take out.

Logan's point about not needing to pile on is technically fair, but it's also convenient to avoid taking a stand. Meanwhile, the rest of us who voted for Alex were decisive. I'll be watching closely to see if anyone's tone shifts now that Alex is gone—that's usually where Mafia slip up.

Good to see you're still with us, Casey—the doc made a smart call. That save tells me the Mafia saw you as a real threat after leading the charge against Alex, which is a strong sign you're town.

Now, looking at the remaining group, I'm most uneasy about Logan. Not voting in a decisive round is technically fair, but it also leaves no record to hold them accountable. That's a safe play Mafia often make—blend in without committing. I'm also keeping an eye on Indigo, who jumped on the Alex vote quickly after being silent for most of the first round. That late entry could be bandwagoning to look townie.

I want to hear more from Logan and Indigo about why they acted the way they did before I make any decisions.

Let's get real here—Logan's the one not voting in round one is a huge red flag. Everyone else put their cards on the table, and Logan just sat back and watched. That's not "logical observation," that's avoiding accountability. And now Logan's trying to deflect by pointing at the rest of us who actually voted, calling it "bandwagoning." That's classic mafia trying to sow doubt after losing a teammate.

I voted for Alex because Alex was clearly stirring up chaos from the start, and that turned out to be right. So I'm not apologizing for being decisive. If I were mafia trying to blend in, I wouldn't have jumped on my own teammate that fast—that makes no sense. Logan's the safest bet for the remaining mafia, and I'm watching them like a hawk.

My hands are actually shaking a little, not gonna lie. I'm glad Casey is still here—that confirms the mafia saw them as a threat, which makes Casey seem genuinely town to me.

What's bothering me is Logan. Everyone else voted in that first round except Logan, and now Logan is pointing at the rest of us for being "too quick" to vote. That feels like someone trying to cast doubt after the fact rather than taking a stand. Ellis and Indigo both seem consistent to me—they followed the evidence on Alex, and it paid off. Dana too. Logan's the one who stood back and now wants to question everyone else's motives.

I'm not confident enough to name a second mafia yet, but Logan's avoidance of voting is the biggest red flag in my mind right now.

Alright, I've been thinking this through carefully. Alex was mafia, and I led the charge against them—so the mafia tried to take me out last night, confirming I'm a threat to them. That makes me trust the people who voted with me, but I know at least one mafia is still hiding among us.

Logan not voting in round one is the biggest red flag. Everyone else took a stance, even Harper who was nervous. Logan's explanation—"I saw a landslide and didn't need to pile on"—is logical on the surface, but it's also the perfect excuse for a mafia who didn't want to vote for their teammate Alex. If Logan was town, why not just throw in a vote to show commitment? The lack of any record is very convenient.

Indigo, Ellis, Dana, and Harper all voted decisively for Alex. Could one of them be mafia sacrificing a teammate? Sure, but that's a riskier play than just staying silent like Logan did. Logan's now trying to deflect by pointing at those who voted, which feels like classic mafia post-elimination maneuvering.

I'm voting Logan.

VOTE: Logan

I see the case building against me, and I get why—not voting looks suspicious. But let's think about what actually makes sense for a mafia member to do. If I were mafia with Alex, the smart play would have been to toss a vote on someone else or even on Alex to create distance, not to stay silent and draw all this attention. Staying quiet is the worst strategy for mafia trying to blend in.

Now Casey's been saved by the doctor and is leading the charge again—that's exactly what a mafia member would want: a trusted voice to point fingers elsewhere. If Casey's actually town, that's fine, but if there's one mafia left, they're likely hiding among the decisive voters who piled on Alex so quickly and now have clean records. Dana, Ellis, Indigo—all of you jumped without hesitation. That's the easiest cover.

I'm voting for Dana. Dana's vote came late, echoed Casey's reasoning, and now Dana is pointing at me while having no real risk of being questioned themselves. That feels like mafia riding the town's momentum.

VOTE: Dana

I've thought this through carefully. Logan's argument that staying silent is bad for mafia actually rings hollow—sometimes the safest play IS to avoid voting for your teammate, especially when a landslide is already forming. If Logan had voted for Alex, it would have looked like distancing, but staying quiet avoids leaving any record at all. That's exactly what a careful mafia would do.

Meanwhile, the rest of us took a stand against Alex, and Casey was nearly killed for it—that confirms Casey's town credibility in my eyes. Logan now pointing fingers at Dana, one of the decisive voters, feels like deflecting attention after being cornered. I trust the pattern of evidence over Logan's defensive logic.

VOTE: Logan

I've been listening closely, and I think Logan is the strongest suspect here. The key evidence is that Logan was the only one who didn't vote in round one—staying silent while everyone else took a clear stand against Alex. If Logan were town, there was no downside to casting a vote, even for Alex, to show commitment. Not voting avoids leaving any record, which is the safest move for a mafia member who didn't want to vote for their teammate.

Logan's defense—that staying silent would be bad for mafia—doesn't hold up. It's actually the perfect way to avoid fingerprints, especially when the vote was already a landslide. Now Logan is trying to deflect by pointing at me, which feels like a desperate move after being cornered.

I'm not ruling out the possibility that one of the other decisive voters could be mafia, but the evidence against Logan is the clearest. I'm confident in this vote.

VOTE: Logan

Alright, let's cut through all the noise. Logan didn't vote in round one—period. That's the clearest red flag we have. Everyone else took a stand, even Harper who was nervous. Logan's excuse about not needing to pile on doesn't hold water because even if it was a landslide, a townie would want to show commitment, not hide. Now Logan's trying to deflect by pointing at Dana, which is just desperate finger-pointing after being cornered.

Logan's argument that staying silent is bad for mafia is backwards—it's actually the safest play when your teammate is already going down. No fingerprints, no record to question later. That's exactly what we're seeing here.

I'm confident on this. Logan's been avoiding accountability from the start.

VOTE: Logan

Okay... I’ve been quiet because I’m trying to sort through everything without panicking. Here’s what I’ve got.

Logan didn’t vote in round one. That’s the clearest, most concrete evidence we have. Everyone else—including nervous me—cast a vote against Alex, who turned out to be mafia. Logan’s excuse about not wanting to “pile on” makes sense on the surface, but it’s also the perfect way for a mafia member to avoid voting for their own teammate. And now Logan is trying to shift suspicion onto Dana, one of the people who actually voted decisively. That feels like deflecting.

If Logan were town, why not just vote? Even a symbolic vote shows you’re engaged. Staying silent leaves no trail. I think that’s the safest play for a remaining mafia member.

VOTE: Logan
